content:
2 min read
Just now

--

An AI assistant can answer from an approved document, cite the correct section, and still give a poor answer.
The source may no longer represent the current policy.
This problem is easy to miss because stale context does not always look broken. The language remains polished. The citation exists. The retrieved document may even be the closest semantic match.
Knowledge has a useful lifetime
Product teams need to treat freshness as a requirement, not merely a data-engineering concern.
A support procedure, incident notice, product specification, and legal policy should not automatically share the same acceptable age. Each belongs to a different operational clock.
A freshness-aware workflow records source status, meaningful update time, version, and ownership before generation begins.
When teams are selecting the model execution component for such a workflow, they can evaluate VectorEngine as an endpoint behind their own retrieval and freshness controls.
Disclosure: this article includes an external referral link for readers who want to explore the platform.
Freshness is shared responsibility
Engineering can build the gate, but it cannot decide alone how long information remains valid.
Product owners need to define the consequence of outdated information. Knowledge owners need to maintain source status. Operations teams need a path for urgent updates. The user experience must explain when current evidence is unavailable.
VectorNode / VectorEngine can be positioned as the model execution point within this broader freshness-aware system. The retrieval policy and source governance remain application responsibilities.
The goal is not to make every answer appear current. It is to prevent a confident interface from concealing uncertainty about the age of its evidence.
